plan.contextChunk:
One classical example of the limitations of motion estimation from
images is the **aperture problem**. The aperture problem happens when
observing the motion of a one-dimensional (1D) structure larger than the
image frame, as shown in @fig-apperture_problem. If none of the
termination points are in view within the observation window (i.e., the
aperture), it is impossible to measure the actual image motion. Only the
component orthogonal to the moving structure can be measured.

![Aperture problem when observing the motion of a one-dimensional (1D) structure larger than the image frame. The actual motion of the bar is upward, but the perception, when vision is limited to what is visible within the observation window, appears as if the motion of the bar is in the direction perpendicular to the bar.](figures/optical_flow/apperture_problem.png){width="100%" #fig-apperture_problem}


:::{.column-margin}
The **Barber-pole illusion** is an illustration of the aperture problem. The barber pole induces the illusion of downward motion while rotating.

![](figures/optical_flow/barber_pole.png){width="40%"}
:::


### Representation of Optical Flow

We can model motion over time as a sequence of **dense optical flow**
images: $$\left[ u(x,y,t), v(x,y,t) \right]$$ The quantities $u(x,y,t)$and $v(x,y,t)$ indicate that a pixel at image coordinates $(x,y)$ at
time $t$ moves with velocity $(u,v)$. The problem with this formulation
is that we do not have an explicit representation of the trajectory
followed by points in the scene over time. As time $t$ changes, the
location $(x,y)$ might correspond to different scene elements.

An alternative representation is to model motion as a **sparse set of
moving points** (tracks):
$$\left\{ \mathbf{P}^{(i)}_t \right\}_{i=1}^N$$ This has the challenge

elements: vertical 3D cylinder representing the barber pole
helical stripe pattern (alternating red, white, and blue bands) mapped as a continuous texture/shader on the cylinder
top and bottom circular caps for the cylinder
curved 3D arrow (semi-circular) hovering above the pole indicating 'Real motion' (horizontal rotation)
straight 3D arrow pointing downwards next to the pole indicating 'Observed motion' (apparent translation)
interactive 'Aperture circular window' overlaying the front of the cylinder, highlighting a localized patch
orthogonal normal vector arrow inside the aperture showing the local perpendicular motion component

interactions.label: Rotation Speed
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates how physical horizontal rotation drives the vertical optical flow illusion; reversing the rotation direction immediately reverses the apparent downward motion to upward

interactions.label: Show Local Aperture
interactions.teaches: Isolates a small circular patch of the pole to show that without boundary cues, local detectors can only measure motion perpendicular to the stripe (the aperture problem)

demo_steps.title: The barber pole illusion
demo_steps.narration: The cylinder is physically rotating horizontally (Real motion). However, because the helical stripes are continuous, your visual system tracks the edges downward, creating a strong illusion of vertical translation (Observed motion).

demo_steps.title: The local aperture problem
demo_steps.narration: Enable the local aperture. By restricting the view to a small window that hides the cylinder's top and bottom boundaries, the diagonal stripes look like infinite 1D lines. Local motion sensors can only detect motion perpendicular to these lines.

demo_steps.title: Reversing physical motion
demo_steps.narration: Reverse the cylinder's rotation. Now, the physical rotation is to the left, which shifts the diagonal helix edges upward. Inside the local aperture, the perpendicular motion component flips, making the entire pole appear to move upward.

demo_steps.title: Stationary ambiguity
demo_steps.narration: Stop the rotation. When the pole is stationary, the spatial orientation of the stripes is clear, but no motion cues exist. The aperture problem is a fundamental limitation of local differential optical flow algorithms (like Lucas-Kanade).

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Slightly elevated orthographic front view, making the cylinder's top cap visible as a shallow ellipse, matching the original layout's 3D depth cue.
interactionPlan.view_reasoning: (1) The dominant axis of the figure is the vertical cylinder running up-down (Y-axis). (2) The cylinder is positioned front-and-center, implying an azimuth of 0 degrees (looking straight at its front face). (3) The top circular cap of the cylinder is visible as a shallow ellipse, which provides a foreshortening cue indicating that the camera is elevated slightly above the horizontal plane, roughly 15 degrees.

camera_view.view_notes: The camera looks slightly down at the vertical cylinder to reveal the elliptical top cap, mirroring the 3D perspective of the textbook sketch while preserving straight vertical lines.

inline_constraints.default_view: must preserve the aspect ratio, showing the tall, narrow barber pole with its circular motion arrow on top and translation arrow on the side
inline_constraints.visible_ui: at most 2 compact controls located near the bottom edge, keeping the upper area clear for the rotation label
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips over the 'Real motion' and 'Observed motion' arrows should explain the physical vs. perceived velocity vectors
inline_constraints.framing: narrow vertical framing, matching the source image layout to prevent empty side whitespace
inline_constraints.camera: must render the cylinder as a clean vertical form with visible top curvature

interactionPlan.notes: To render the stripes smoothly, use a custom fragment shader on the CylinderGeometry that calculates a helical color pattern based on (y + speed * time + continuous_angle). When 'apertureView' is true, dim the rest of the cylinder with a semi-transparent dark overlay, leaving only a small circular stencil window fully bright and rendering a prominent local normal arrow within it.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a vertical cylinder with diagonal stripes from a front-on orthographic perspective, with flat circular caps at the top and bottom. A curved arrow is situated directly above the top face, and a straight downward arrow is to its right.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot shows the cylinder from a front-on orthographic view matching the source, with a curved horizontal arrow above and a straight vertical arrow on the right.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The viewing angles match perfectly, capturing the orthographic, 2D-like projection of the 3D scene.
discrepancies: The 'Real motion' text label is missing or cut off at the top of the viewport.
The arrowhead of the 'Real motion' curved arrow is distorted into a round blob instead of a sharp pointer.
The 'Observed motion' arrow shaft slightly overlaps with the text 'motion' above it.
evaluation.notes: Excellent replication of the illusion and aperture mechanics, but the top label is cut off and the top arrowhead geometry is blobby.
action_items: Adjust the camera view or label offset so that the 'Real motion' label is fully visible and not cropped out of the top frame.
Fix the arrowhead of the curved 'Real motion' arrow to use a properly oriented, crisp cone geometry instead of a blob.
Slightly shift the 'Observed motion' arrow downwards or increase the label offset so they do not overlap.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual framework and interactivity are highly accurate (5/5), but minor execution issues like cropped labels, overlapping text, and a distorted curved arrowhead need to be refined in the generation phase.
action_items: Adjust the camera view or label offset so that the 'Real motion' label is fully visible and not cropped out of the top frame.
Fix the arrowhead of the curved 'Real motion' arrow to use a properly oriented, crisp cone geometry instead of a blob.
Slightly shift the 'Observed motion' arrow downwards or increase the label offset so they do not overlap.
